name: Grenada
excerpt: The indigenous Carib people inhabited Grenada when Christopher COLUMBUS landed on the island in 1498, but it remained uncolonized for more than a century. The French settled Grenada in the 17th century, established sugar estates, and imported large numbers of African slaves.

quick_facts.government: parliamentary democracy under a constitutional monarchy; a Commonwealth realm
quick_facts.religions: Protestant 49.2% (includes Pentecostal 17.2%, Seventh Day Adventist 13.2%, Anglican 8.5%, Baptist 3.2%, Church of God 2.4%, Evangelical 1.9%, Methodist 1.6%, other 1.2%), Roman Catholic 36%, Jehovah's Witness 1.2%, Rastafarian 1.2%, other 5.5%, none 5.7%, unspecified 1.3% (2011 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: African descent 82.4%, mixed 13.3%, East Indian 2.2%, other 1.3%, unspecified 0.9% (2011 est.)
quick_facts.climate: tropical; tempered by northeast trade winds
quick_facts.terrain: volcanic in origin with central mountains
quick_facts.major_exports: nutmeg/cardamons, fish, wheat flours, frozen fruits and nuts, aqueous paints (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: Location: Caribbean, island between the Caribbean Sea and Atlantic Ocean, north of Trinidad and Tobago
summaries.history_summary: The indigenous Carib people inhabited Grenada when Christopher COLUMBUS landed on the island in 1498, but it remained uncolonized for more than a century. The French settled Grenada in the 17th century, established sugar estates, and imported large numbers of African slaves. Britain took the island in 1762 and vigorously expanded sugar production. In the 19th century, cacao eventually surpassed sugar as the main export crop; in the 20th century, nutmeg became the leading export. In 1967, Britain gave Grenada autonomy over its internal affairs. Full independence was attained in 1974, making Grenada one of the smallest independent countries in the Western Hemisphere. In 1979, a leftist New Jewel Movement seized power under Maurice BISHOP, ushering in the Grenada Revolution. On 19 October 1983, factions within the revolutionary government overthrew and killed BISHOP and members of his party. Six days later, US forces and those of six other Caribbean nations intervened, quickly capturing the ringleaders and their hundreds of Cuban advisers. Rule of law was restored, and democratic elections were reinstituted the following year and have continued since.
summaries.government_summary: parliamentary democracy under a constitutional monarchy; a Commonwealth realm. Independence: 7 February 1974 (from the UK)
summaries.economy_summary: small OECS service-based economy; large tourism, construction, transportation, and education sectors; major spice exporter; shrinking but still high public debt; vulnerable to hurricanes; emerging blue economy incentives

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Grenada has a substantial Protestant population, including an existing Baptist witness, alongside its Catholic minority. Pray for these evangelical churches to be strengthened in sound biblical doctrine.
prayer_points: Pray for Grenada's existing Baptist and Protestant churches to be strengthened and equipped with sound biblical teaching.
Pray for pastors to be trained in expository, Scripture-centered preaching.
Pray for Catholics practicing inherited religious tradition to encounter genuine salvation by grace through faith in Christ.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Grenada's substantial Protestant population, including an existing Baptist presence, is real ground to build on. Ongoing needs include strengthening biblical literacy among existing churches and reaching the island's Catholic population with the clear gospel.
